news_summary: Today's space news highlights exciting advances in understanding the universe, from uncovering potential signs of alien life and mapping vast galactic ice to creating the largest cosmic map of millions of galaxies, while the Artemis II mission inspires global unity through lunar exploration.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées passionnantes dans la compréhension de l'univers, allant de la découverte de signes potentiels de vie extraterrestre et de la cartographie de vastes étendues de glace galactique à la création de la plus grande carte cosmique regroupant des millions de galaxies, tandis que la mission Artemis II inspire l'unité mondiale à travers l'exploration lunaire.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan avances apasionantes en la comprensión del universo, desde el descubrimiento de posibles indicios de vida extraterrestre y la cartografía de vastas extensiones de hielo galáctico hasta la creación del mayor mapa cósmico de millones de galaxias, mientras que la misión Artemis II inspira la unidad mundial a través de la exploración lunar.

articles.title: Scientists think alien life might be hiding in patterns
articles.summary: A new study proposes detecting life in space by spotting patterns across many planets instead of focusing on one at a time. If life spreads and changes planetary environments, it could leave behind statistical clues linking planets together. These patterns may reveal life even when traditional biosignatures are unclear or misleading. The method could help scientists prioritize which planets are most likely to host life.
articles.content:
A team led by Specially Appointed Associate Professor Harrison B. Smith of the Earth-Life Science Institute (ELSI) at the Institute of Science Tokyo and Specially Appointed Associate Professor Lana Sinapayen of the National Institute for Basic Biology has introduced a new strategy for finding life beyond Earth. Instead of searching for specific biological signals, their approach looks for patterns shared across groups of planets. This idea offers a fresh direction for astrobiology, especially in cases where traditional biosignatures are unclear or unreliable.

One of the biggest challenges in the search for extraterrestrial life is determining whether features observed on distant planets truly point to living organisms. Common biosignatures, such as certain gases in a planet's atmosphere, can sometimes be produced by non-living processes, leading to false positives. Technosignatures may be more convincing, but they depend on assumptions about how intelligent life might behave, which adds uncertainty.

To address these issues, the researchers explored a different perspective. Instead of focusing on individual planets, they asked whether life could be identified through its broader effects across many worlds.

An "Agnostic Biosignature" Approach

The team introduces the concept of an "agnostic biosignature," which avoids relying on detailed knowledge of what life is or how it operates. This method is built on two general ideas: that life can move between planets (for example, through panspermia), and that it gradually changes the environments it inhabits.

To test this concept, the researchers used an agent-based simulation to model how life might spread across star systems and influence planetary properties. Their results show that if life spreads and alters planets, it can create measurable statistical links between where planets are located and what characteristics they display.

Importantly, these patterns can emerge even when no single planet shows a clear biosignature.

Detecting Life Through Planetary Patterns

In addition to identifying the presence of life, the team developed a way to pinpoint which planets are most likely to host it. By grouping planets based on shared features and their positions in space, they were able to identify clusters that are more likely to have been shaped by biological activity.

This method emphasizes accuracy over completeness. It is designed to reduce false positives, even if that means some life-bearing planets are overlooked. This trade-off is valuable when telescope time is limited and follow-up observations must be carefully chosen.

A New Direction for Astrobiology Research

"By focusing on how life spreads and interacts with environments, we can search for it without needing a perfect definition or a single definitive signal," said Harrison B. Smith. Lana Sinapayen added, "Even if life elsewhere is fundamentally different from life on Earth, its large-scale effects, such as spreading and modifying planets, may still leave detectable traces. That's what makes this approach compelling."

The findings suggest that future surveys, which will examine large numbers of exoplanets, could use statistical techniques to detect life across entire populations of planets. This could be especially helpful when individual signals are weak, unclear, or easily misinterpreted.

Looking Ahead

The study also points out the need to better understand the natural variety of planets that form without life. Having a clearer baseline will make it easier to recognize unusual patterns that might be caused by biological processes.

Although the current research is based on simulations, it lays the groundwork for a new class of life-detection methods. The team notes that future studies will need to incorporate more detailed planetary data and realistic models of how galaxies evolve. Even so, the results indicate that life might be identified not by its chemistry alone, but by the large-scale patterns it leaves across the universe.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a novel scientific approach with broad implications for detecting extraterrestrial life, potentially transforming astrobiology and guiding future space exploration efforts. It presents a clear, detailed concept with significant real-world impact for humanity's understanding of life in the universe.
articles.personality_title: Scientists propose finding alien life by spotting patterns across many planets
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long searched for signs of life on other planets by looking for specific clues, like certain gases in atmospheres. But these clues can sometimes be confusing or come from non-living sources.

**What happened** – A team of researchers from Japan introduced a new way to find life beyond Earth. Instead of focusing on one planet at a time, they suggest looking for patterns shared by groups of planets. They used computer simulations to show that if life spreads between planets and changes their environments, it could create links visible in large sets of planets.

**Impact** – This method is important because it does not rely on knowing exactly what alien life looks like. It can find signs of life even when traditional clues are unclear. It also helps scientists decide which planets to study more closely, saving time and resources.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to improve their models with more detailed planet data and better understand how planets form without life. Future space surveys could use these pattern methods to scan many planets and spot possible signs of life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists are exploring a new way to detect alien life by finding shared patterns across many planets, which could change how we search for life in space.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques proposent de détecter la vie extraterrestre en repérant des motifs sur plusieurs planètes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent depuis longtemps des signes de vie sur d'autres planètes en recherchant des indices spécifiques, comme certains gaz dans les atmosphères. Mais ces indices peuvent parfois prêter à confusion ou provenir de sources non vivantes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs japonais a présenté une nouvelle méthode pour trouver la vie au-delà de la Terre. Au lieu de se concentrer sur une seule planète, ils proposent d’observer des motifs communs à plusieurs planètes. Ils ont utilisé des simulations informatiques pour montrer que si la vie se propage entre les planètes et modifie leur environnement, cela peut créer des liens visibles dans un grand groupe de planètes.

**Impact** – Cette méthode est importante car elle ne dépend pas de la connaissance exacte de ce à quoi ressemble la vie extraterrestre. Elle peut détecter des signes de vie même lorsque les indices traditionnels sont flous. Elle aide aussi les scientifiques à choisir quelles planètes étudier en priorité, ce qui économise du temps et des ressources.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les chercheurs veulent améliorer leurs modèles avec des données planétaires plus précises et mieux comprendre comment les planètes se forment sans vie. Les futures études spatiales pourraient utiliser cette méthode de motifs pour analyser de nombreuses planètes et détecter des signes possibles de vie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques explorent une nouvelle façon de détecter la vie extraterrestre en trouvant des motifs communs à plusieurs planètes, ce qui pourrait changer notre manière de chercher la vie dans l’espace.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos proponen encontrar vida alienígena detectando patrones en varios planetas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han buscado señales de vida en otros planetas observando pistas específicas, como ciertos gases en las atmósferas. Pero estas pistas a veces pueden ser confusas o tener orígenes no vivos.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigadores japoneses presentó una nueva forma de encontrar vida más allá de la Tierra. En lugar de enfocarse en un solo planeta, sugieren buscar patrones compartidos por grupos de planetas. Usaron simulaciones por computadora para mostrar que si la vida se propaga entre planetas y cambia sus ambientes, esto puede crear conexiones visibles en grandes grupos de planetas.

**Impacto** – Este método es importante porque no depende de saber exactamente cómo es la vida alienígena. Puede encontrar señales de vida incluso cuando las pistas tradicionales no son claras. También ayuda a los científicos a decidir qué planetas estudiar primero, ahorrando tiempo y recursos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren mejorar sus modelos con datos planetarios más detallados y comprender mejor cómo se forman los planetas sin vida. Las futuras exploraciones espaciales podrían usar estos métodos de patrones para analizar muchos planetas y detectar posibles signos de vida.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los científicos exploran una nueva forma de detectar vida alienígena encontrando patrones compartidos en varios planetas, lo que podría cambiar cómo buscamos vida en el espacio.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a cluster of diverse planets connected by glowing, flowing patterns of light that symbolize the spread of life between them, set against a softly colored cosmic background with subtle hints of star systems and abstract, wave-like environmental changes enveloping the planets.

articles.title: ‘Interstellar Glaciers’: NASA’s SPHEREx Maps Vast Galactic Ice Regions
articles.summary: NASA’s SPHEREx (Spectro-Photometer for the History of the Universe, Epoch of Reionization, and Ices Explorer) mission has mapped interstellar ice at an unprecedented scale. Covering regions in our Milky Way galaxy more than 600 light-years across, the ice was found inside giant molecular clouds — vast regions of gas and dust where dense clumps of […]
articles.content:
Water ice highlighted Interstellar dust highlighted These observations made by NASA’s SPHEREx mission reveal vast frozen complexes in the Cygnus X star-forming region of the Milky Way galaxy. Water ice, shown as bright blue structures at left, exactly overlays the dark lanes of interstellar dust, shown in different wavelengths at right. NASA/JPL-Caltech/IPAC/Hora et al These observations made by NASA’s SPHEREx mission reveal vast frozen complexes in the Cygnus X star-forming region of the Milky Way galaxy. Water ice, shown as bright blue structures at left, exactly overlays the dark lanes of interstellar dust, shown in different wavelengths at right. Water ice highlighted Interstellar dust highlighted These observations made by NASA’s SPHEREx mission reveal vast frozen complexes in the Cygnus X star-forming region of the Milky Way galaxy. Water ice, shown as bright blue structures at left, exactly overlays the dark lanes of interstellar dust, shown in different wavelengths at right. NASA/JPL-Caltech/IPAC/Hora et al These observations made by NASA’s SPHEREx mission reveal vast frozen complexes in the Cygnus X star-forming region of the Milky Way galaxy. Water ice, shown as bright blue structures at left, exactly overlays the dark lanes of interstellar dust, shown in different wavelengths at right.

Water ice highlighted

Interstellar dust highlighted

Curtain Toggle 2-Up Image Details These observations made by NASA’s SPHEREx mission reveal vast frozen complexes in the Cygnus X star-forming region of the Milky Way galaxy. Water ice, shown as bright blue structures at left, exactly overlays the dark lanes of interstellar dust, shown in different wavelengths at right.

NASA’s SPHEREx (Spectro-Photometer for the History of the Universe, Epoch of Reionization, and Ices Explorer) mission has mapped interstellar ice at an unprecedented scale. Covering regions in our Milky Way galaxy more than 600 light-years across, the ice was found inside giant molecular clouds — vast regions of gas and dust where dense clumps of matter collapse under gravity, giving birth to stars. A study describing these findings published Wednesday in The Astrophysical Journal.

One of SPHEREx’s main goals is to map the chemical signatures of various types of interstellar ice. This ice includes molecules like water, carbon dioxide, and carbon monoxide, which are vital to the chemistry that allows life to develop. Researchers believe these ice reservoirs, attached to the surfaces of tiny dust grains, are where most of the universe’s water is formed and stored. The water in Earth’s oceans — and the ices in comets and on other planets and moons in our galaxy — originates from these regions.

“These vast frozen complexes are like ‘interstellar glaciers’ that could deliver a massive water supply to new solar systems that will be born in the region,” said study coauthor Phil Korngut, the instrument scientist for SPHEREx at Caltech in Pasadena, California. “It’s a profound idea that we are looking at a map of material that could rain on nascent planets and potentially support future life.”

Thanks to its spectral capabilities, SPHEREx can measure the amounts of various ices and molecules, such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, in and around molecular clouds, helping scientists better understand their composition and environment.

Although space telescopes such as NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope and the agency’s retired Spitzer have detected water, carbon dioxide, carbon monoxide, and other icy molecules throughout our galaxy, the SPHEREx observatory is the first infrared mission specifically designed to find such molecules over the entire sky via the mission’s large-scale spectral survey.

“We expected to detect these ices in front of individual bright stars: The light from a star acts like a spotlight, revealing any ice in the space between us and that star. But this is something different,” said lead author Joseph Hora, an astronomer at the Center for Astrophysics (CfA) at Harvard & Smithsonian in Cambridge, Massachusetts. “When looking along the galactic plane — where most of the stars, gas, and dust of our galaxy are concentrated — there’s a lot of diffuse background light shining through entire dust clouds, and SPHEREx can see the spatial distribution of the ices they contain in incredible detail.”

Managed by NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California, the SPHEREx observatory launched March 11, 2025, and has the unique ability to see the sky in 102 colors, each representing a different wavelength of infrared light that offers distinctive information about galaxies, stars, planet-forming regions, and other cosmic features. By late 2025, SPHEREx had completed the first of four all-sky infrared maps of the universe, charting the positions of hundreds of millions of galaxies in 3D to help answer major questions about the cosmos, including those about the origins of water and life.

Icy origins

Using the SPHEREx maps of various icy molecules, the study’s authors were able to look deep into many molecular clouds in the Cygnus X and North American Nebula regions of the Milky Way. In the densest areas, where the amount of dust is greatest, dark filamentary lanes block the visible light from the stars behind. With its infrared eye, the space telescope also revealed where the different ices — which absorb specific wavelengths of infrared light that would pass through the clouds if they consisted only of dust — are at their densest.

This finding supports the hypothesis that interstellar ice forms on the surface of tiny dust particles, which are no larger than particles found in candle smoke, and that the dense regions of dust shield the ices from the intense ultraviolet radiation emitted by newborn stars. However, not all ices are treated the same way in the interstellar medium.

“We can investigate the environmental factors that contribute to different ice formation rates across large areas of interstellar space,” said study coauthor Gary Melnick, also an astronomer at the CfA. “The SPHEREx mission’s ‘big picture’ view provides valuable new information you can’t get when zooming in on a small region.”

Within this broad perspective, adds Melnick, SPHEREx can do something ground-based observatories cannot: detect varying amounts of water and carbon dioxide, two ices that respond differently to environmental factors. For example, the presence of intense ultraviolet light from nearby massive young stars or the heating of these dust grains by that light affects the abundances of different ices in distinct ways.

This is just the beginning for the mission. Observations from SPHEREx will provide scientists with a powerful tool to explore the various components of our galaxy, the physics of the interstellar medium that lead to star and planet formation, and the chemical processes that deliver molecules essential for life to newly formed planets.

More about SPHEREx

The mission is managed by JPL for the agency’s Astrophysics Division within the Science Mission Directorate in Washington. The telescope and the spacecraft bus were built by BAE Systems in Boulder, Colorado. The science analysis of the SPHEREx data is being conducted by a team of scientists at 13 institutions across the U.S. and in South Korea and Taiwan, led by Principal Investigator Jamie Bock, who is based at Caltech with a joint JPL appointment, and by JPL Project Scientist Olivier Doré. Data is processed and archived at IPAC at Caltech in Pasadena, which manages JPL for NASA. The SPHEREx dataset is freely available to scientists and the public.

For more information about the SPHEREx mission visit:

https://science.nasa.gov/mission/spherex/

Media Contacts

Ian J. O’Neill

Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Pasadena, Calif.

818-354-2649

ian.j.oneill@jpl.nasa.gov

Alise Fisher

NASA Headquarters, Washington

202-617-4977

alise.m.fisher@nasa.gov

Amy C. Oliver, FRAS

Public Affairs Officer

Smithsonian Astrophysical Observatory

amy.oliver@cfa.harvard.edu

2026-022
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific achievement by NASA's SPHEREx mission mapping interstellar ice on an unprecedented scale, revealing vast frozen complexes in star-forming regions of the Milky Way. This discovery has broad implications for understanding the origins of water and life in the universe, providing valuable insights into star and planet formation. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a breakthrough with meaningful impact for science and humanity's knowledge of cosmic origins.
articles.personality_title: NASA's SPHEREx mission maps vast ice clouds in our galaxy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA launched the SPHEREx mission in March 2025 to study the universe in infrared light. One of its goals is to find and map interstellar ices, like water and carbon dioxide, which exist in space inside giant clouds of gas and dust where stars are born.

**What happened** – SPHEREx has created the first large-scale map of icy molecules across wide areas in the Milky Way galaxy, covering regions more than 600 light-years across. The mission detected water ice and other frozen molecules inside giant molecular clouds, especially in star-forming areas like Cygnus X. These ices cling to tiny dust grains and are important because they contain much of the universe’s water.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have been able to see how these ices are spread out over huge parts of our galaxy, rather than just near individual stars. It helps us understand where water and other life-related molecules come from in space. Since these icy regions may supply water to new planets forming around young stars, the findings offer clues about how planets like Earth get their water.

**What's next step** – SPHEREx will continue to create more detailed maps of interstellar ices and molecules across the sky. Scientists will use this data to study how different environments affect ice formation and to learn more about how stars and planets develop in our galaxy.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s SPHEREx mission has mapped huge icy regions in the Milky Way, revealing where water and other vital molecules exist in space and how they may help form new planets.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission SPHEREx de la NASA cartographie d’immenses nuages de glace dans notre galaxie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA a lancé la mission SPHEREx en mars 2025 pour étudier l’univers en lumière infrarouge. L’un de ses objectifs est de détecter et de cartographier les glaces interstellaires, comme l’eau et le dioxyde de carbone, qui se trouvent dans l’espace à l’intérieur de grands nuages de gaz et de poussière où naissent les étoiles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – SPHEREx a créé la première carte à grande échelle des molécules glacées dans de vastes régions de la galaxie de la Voie lactée, couvrant plus de 600 années-lumière. La mission a détecté de la glace d’eau et d’autres molécules gelées dans d’énormes nuages moléculaires, notamment dans des zones de formation d’étoiles comme Cygnus X. Ces glaces adhèrent à de minuscules grains de poussière et contiennent une grande partie de l’eau de l’univers.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que les scientifiques peuvent voir comment ces glaces sont réparties sur d’immenses parties de notre galaxie, au-delà des étoiles individuelles. Cela aide à comprendre d’où vient l’eau et d’autres molécules importantes pour la vie dans l’espace. Ces régions glacées pourraient fournir de l’eau aux nouvelles planètes en formation autour de jeunes étoiles.

**Prochaine étape** – SPHEREx continuera à réaliser des cartes plus détaillées des glaces interstellaires et des molécules dans tout le ciel. Les scientifiques utiliseront ces données pour étudier comment différents environnements influencent la formation de glace et pour mieux comprendre la formation des étoiles et des planètes dans notre galaxie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La mission SPHEREx de la NASA a cartographié d’immenses régions glacées dans la Voie lactée, révélant où se trouvent l’eau et d’autres molécules essentielles dans l’espace et comment elles pourraient aider à former de nouvelles planètes.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión SPHEREx de la NASA mapea grandes nubes de hielo en nuestra galaxia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA lanzó la misión SPHEREx en marzo de 2025 para estudiar el universo en luz infrarroja. Uno de sus objetivos es detectar y mapear hielos interestelares, como agua y dióxido de carbono, que existen en el espacio dentro de grandes nubes de gas y polvo donde nacen las estrellas.

**Qué pasó** – SPHEREx creó el primer mapa a gran escala de moléculas heladas en amplias áreas de la galaxia Vía Láctea, cubriendo regiones de más de 600 años luz. La misión detectó hielo de agua y otras moléculas congeladas dentro de grandes nubes moleculares, especialmente en zonas donde se forman estrellas como Cygnus X. Estos hielos se adhieren a pequeños granos de polvo y contienen gran parte del agua del universo.

**Impacto** – Por primera vez, los científicos pueden ver cómo estos hielos se distribuyen en grandes partes de nuestra galaxia, no solo cerca de estrellas individuales. Esto ayuda a entender de dónde proviene el agua y otras moléculas importantes para la vida en el espacio. Estas regiones heladas podrían suministrar agua a nuevos planetas que se están formando.

**Próximo paso** – SPHEREx continuará creando mapas más detallados de hielos interestelares y moléculas en todo el cielo. Los científicos usarán estos datos para estudiar cómo diferentes entornos afectan la formación de hielo y para aprender más sobre cómo se forman las estrellas y planetas en nuestra galaxia.

**Resumen en una frase** – La misión SPHEREx de la NASA ha mapeado grandes regiones heladas en la Vía Láctea, mostrando dónde existen agua y otras moléculas vitales en el espacio y cómo podrían ayudar a formar nuevos planetas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vast cosmic landscape showing glowing bright blue crystalline ice formations delicately overlaying intricate dark, dusty filaments weaving through a star-forming nebula, symbolizing interstellar glaciers within the Cygnus X molecular clouds, with soft, natural colors highlighting the interplay of frozen water ice and interstellar dust under a gentle cosmic glow.

articles.title: Largest ever map of universe captures 47 million galaxies and quasars
articles.summary: The Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) has finished the most detailed survey of the universe to date, and the resulting map will help researchers understand an apparent weakening of dark energy
articles.content:
The Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) has finished the most detailed survey of the universe to date, and the resulting map will help researchers understand an apparent weakening of dark energy

A thin slice of the map produced by the DESI five-year survey shows galaxies and quasars above and below the plane of the Milky Way, with Earth at the centre Claire Lamman/DESI collaboration

A five-year survey of the sky that has captured more than 47 million galaxies and quasars is now complete, enabling researchers to put the finishing touches on the most detailed map of the universe ever made. The data could help solve the mystery of an apparent weakening of dark energy, which threatens to upend our standard model of the cosmos.

The Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) at Kitt Peak National Observatory in Arizona has been scanning the sky since 2021. Researchers originally expected its survey to gather data on 34 million galaxies and quasars, but DESI surprised researchers with its efficiency. Because of the vast distances involved, some of these extremely faint galaxies have been observed from just 100 or 200 photons.

Advertisement

David Schlegel at the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory in California says our previous maps of the cosmos include a total of 5 million galaxies, so the DESI data increases our knowledge of the universe by a factor of almost 10.

“We’ve actually been on this curve now for my whole career where, every 10 years, we’re making 10-times-larger maps,” he says. “You can ask the question, at what point have you mapped every observable galaxy within 10 billion light years… and if we stayed on the curve, we would do that by 2061.”

The main survey is now complete, but the data will take another year to analyse before it is made available to researchers. The project will continue to collect data for at least another two and a half years, and Schlegel says there are hopes that DESI can be upgraded and kept running well into the 2030s. “This is still the leading instrument like it in the world,” he says.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

DESI’s map now covers 14,000 square degrees of the sky, but the team hopes to expand this to 17,000 square degrees. The full sky has over 41,000 square degrees, but much of that is hard to observe because of relatively close and bright objects, such as our own galaxy, the Milky Way.

The data will allow scientists to compare how galaxies were distributed in the distant past and today. This could lead to insights into the power of dark energy, which makes up about 70 per cent of the universe. An earlier dataset from DESI in 2024 suggested that, rather than remaining constant as expected, dark energy is weakening over time.

If dark energy is indeed weakening, it would have profound implications for the standard model of cosmology, known as lambda-CDM. The full set of DESI data will allow that phenomenon to be investigated further.

Ofer Lahav at University College London says having access to the latest map from DESI would have seemed like science fiction at the start of his career. “When I was a PhD student in Cambridge, 40 years ago, we had a sample of thousands of galaxies. The community was starving for data,” he says. “I think my students [today] may have the opposite problem; to have been flooded with data, and it’s very challenging to analyse it.”

With so much data, there will be scientific breakthroughs about the nature of the universe, says Lahav, but we have also probably caught unusual one-off cosmological incidents that lead to exciting research.
articles.reasoning: The article reports the completion of the most detailed map of the universe ever made, capturing 47 million galaxies and quasars, significantly expanding our cosmic knowledge. This large-scale scientific achievement has broad significance for cosmology and could lead to breakthroughs in understanding dark energy, impacting the standard model of the universe. The detailed context and future research potential provide substantial substance.
articles.personality_title: DESI completes largest-ever map of the universe with 47 million galaxies and quasars
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been mapping the universe by observing galaxies and other distant objects to understand how the cosmos works. Dark energy, a mysterious force making up most of the universe, affects how the universe expands, but its true nature is still unclear.

**What happened** – The Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) finished a five-year survey capturing more than 47 million galaxies and quasars. This is the most detailed map of the universe ever created, covering a large part of the sky. DESI worked faster and better than expected, collecting data from very faint and distant objects.

**Impact** – This new map is nearly ten times larger than previous maps, greatly expanding our knowledge of the universe. It will help scientists study how dark energy has changed over time. Early results suggest dark energy might be weakening, which could change our current understanding of the universe’s history and future.

**What's next step** – Researchers will spend the next year analyzing the full data before sharing it widely. DESI will keep collecting data for several more years and may be upgraded to continue its work into the 2030s. More data will help scientists confirm if dark energy is really changing and reveal new details about the cosmos.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The DESI project has created the largest and most detailed map of the universe, opening new paths to study dark energy and the structure of the cosmos.
articles.personality_title_fr: DESI achève la carte la plus grande jamais réalisée de l’univers avec 47 millions de galaxies et quasars
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cartographient l’univers en observant les galaxies et autres objets lointains pour comprendre son fonctionnement. L’énergie sombre, une force mystérieuse qui compose la majeure partie de l’univers, influence son expansion, mais sa nature reste inconnue.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’instrument spectroscopique Dark Energy (DESI) a terminé une enquête de cinq ans capturant plus de 47 millions de galaxies et quasars. C’est la carte la plus détaillée de l’univers jamais créée, couvrant une grande partie du ciel. DESI a travaillé plus vite et mieux que prévu, recueillant des données d’objets très faibles et lointains.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle carte est presque dix fois plus grande que les cartes précédentes, élargissant considérablement notre connaissance de l’univers. Elle aidera les scientifiques à étudier comment l’énergie sombre a évolué dans le temps. Les premiers résultats suggèrent que l’énergie sombre pourrait s’affaiblir, ce qui pourrait changer notre compréhension actuelle de l’histoire et du futur de l’univers.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs passeront l’année prochaine à analyser les données complètes avant de les partager largement. DESI continuera à collecter des données pendant plusieurs années et pourrait être amélioré pour poursuivre son travail jusque dans les années 2030. Plus de données aideront à confirmer si l’énergie sombre change vraiment et révéleront de nouveaux détails sur le cosmos.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le projet DESI a créé la carte la plus grande et détaillée de l’univers, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour étudier l’énergie sombre et la structure du cosmos.
articles.personality_title_es: DESI completa el mapa más grande del universo con 47 millones de galaxias y cuásares
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estado mapeando el universo observando galaxias y otros objetos distantes para entender cómo funciona el cosmos. La energía oscura, una fuerza misteriosa que compone la mayor parte del universo, afecta su expansión, pero su naturaleza real sigue siendo desconocida.

**Qué pasó** – El instrumento espectroscópico Dark Energy (DESI) terminó una encuesta de cinco años capturando más de 47 millones de galaxias y cuásares. Este es el mapa más detallado del universo jamás creado, cubriendo una gran parte del cielo. DESI trabajó más rápido y mejor de lo esperado, recolectando datos de objetos muy débiles y distantes.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo mapa es casi diez veces más grande que los mapas anteriores, ampliando mucho nuestro conocimiento del universo. Ayudará a los científicos a estudiar cómo ha cambiado la energía oscura con el tiempo. Los primeros resultados sugieren que la energía oscura podría estar debilitándose, lo que podría cambiar nuestra comprensión actual de la historia y el futuro del universo.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores pasarán el próximo año analizando todos los datos antes de compartirlos ampliamente. DESI seguirá recolectando datos durante varios años más y podría ser mejorado para continuar su trabajo hasta la década de 2030. Más datos ayudarán a confirmar si la energía oscura realmente está cambiando y revelarán nuevos detalles sobre el cosmos.

**Resumen en una frase** – El proyecto DESI ha creado el mapa más grande y detallado del universo, abriendo nuevas formas para estudiar la energía oscura y la estructura del cosmos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vast cosmic map unfolding like a glowing parchment in space, centered on a radiant Earth silhouette surrounded by countless shimmering galaxies and quasars represented as delicate clusters of light, with soft gradients of deep blues, purples, and gentle golds symbolizing the expanding universe and the mysterious, weakening dark energy subtly weaving through the scene as a faint, flowing mist.

articles.title: Artemis II shows the moon can still unite a divided world
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM When 15 Girl Scouts in Pilot Mountain, North Carolina, gathered to watch the Artemis II launch, troop leader Heather Willard wasn’t sure how captivated they’d be. Then the rocket lifted off. “All of the girls were mesmerized,” she said. Across the country, the same thing kept happening. For ten […]
The post Artemis II shows the moon can still unite a divided world first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

When 15 Girl Scouts in Pilot Mountain, North Carolina, gathered to watch the Artemis II launch, troop leader Heather Willard wasn’t sure how captivated they’d be. Then the rocket lifted off.

“All of the girls were mesmerized,” she said.

Across the country, the same thing kept happening. For ten days, as Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen flew around the far side of the moon and came back safely, a country that can’t agree on much of anything found itself watching the same thing. The mission ended with splashdown off the coast of San Diego, the first crewed lunar voyage in more than 50 years. It was an aerospace milestone. It was also something that turns out to be surprisingly rare: something almost everyone wanted to see.

A country that disagrees on almost everything agreed on this

About 69 percent of Americans say they get excited about space exploration. Around 80 percent hold a favorable view of NASA, Republicans and Democrats alike, according to a Reuters/Ipsos poll taken during the mission. Nearly 69 percent said it matters to them that astronauts go back to the moon.

At Chicago’s Adler Planetarium, senior astronomer Gaza Gyuk watched hundreds of people come in to track the mission: different backgrounds, different ages, the same room.

“Everyone can be excited about humans extending their capabilities, learning new things, and doing so in a positive, peaceful way,” he said.

In Elkins Park, Pennsylvania, astronomy enthusiast Hector Ybe organized a launch watch party that drew around 225 people: families, kids, people from different ethnic and religious communities. For two hours, he said, the outside world dropped away.

“For two hours, everybody forgot what was happening outside in the world, everybody was talking about space,” Ybe said.

The crew the country needed to see

Part of what made Artemis II feel different was who was on board. Victor Glover was the first Black astronaut to travel to the moon. Christina Koch was the first woman. Commander Reid Wiseman and mission specialist Jeremy Hansen, the first Canadian to fly a lunar mission, rounded out a crew that looked more like the country watching than any Apollo team ever had.

In Northglenn, Colorado, engineering teacher Erin Brabant covered her school hallway with mission posters and had students build their own lunar lander models. Whenever Artemis came up in class, the same thing happened.

“When we talk about Artemis, it’s like every kid stops what they’re doing,” Brabant said. “Their little side conversations stop, and they have questions.”

For the Girl Scouts in North Carolina, Koch meant something specific. She is a former Girl Scout herself, and the troop had been preparing presentations on famous alumnae for Women’s History Month when the launch pulled their attention to her story.

Why the moon still pulls at us

The draw people feel toward the moon goes back further than any space program, and it is not only emotional. Earth and the moon came from the same collision. Around 4.5 billion years ago, a Mars-sized body called Theia slammed into a young Earth, and the debris that coalesced in orbit became the moon. The atoms from that impact are present in every living thing on this planet, the same ones that were in every person who stood outside to watch the launch. The moon has never been foreign to us. It was formed from the same event we were.

It also never stopped shaping how life works here. The moon stirs the oceans, steadied Earth’s axial tilt during the long stretch when complex life was getting started, and its craters hold geological history that wind and rain on Earth have long since erased. Scientists are still reading what is in them. There may be effects that nobody has catalogued yet.

When Gyuk shared images from the Artemis mission showing Earth from deep space, oceans and landmasses clear but no borders in sight, he noticed what happened in the room. “That helps people sort of realize that we’re all in this together,” he said.

“We will always choose Earth”

The moment people keep returning to is what Koch radioed to Mission Control in Houston, looking back at a planet that had become small and distant after the crew’s translunar burn.

“We hear you can look up and see the moon right now. We see you, too,” she said. “When we burned this burn towards the moon, I said that ‘we do not leave Earth, but we choose it.’ And that is true. We will explore. We will build. We will build ships. We will visit again. We will construct science outposts. We will drive rovers. We will do radio astronomy. We will found companies. We will bolster industry. We will inspire. But ultimately we will always choose Earth. We will always choose each other.”

NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman called the splashdown “just the beginning,” with a lunar landing planned for 2028 and a permanent base as the longer-term goal. Whether any of that stays on schedule is an open question. What Artemis II already gave people is harder to plan for: a few days when millions of them stopped arguing and looked at the same sky. The moon has been pulling that off since before anyone thought to write it down.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article describes the successful Artemis II crewed lunar mission, a significant aerospace milestone with broad public engagement and inspiration. It highlights the mission's unifying effect on a divided society, the diverse and representative astronaut crew, and the potential for future exploration and scientific advancement. The story focuses on a single, well-detailed event with meaningful societal and inspirational impact.
articles.personality_title: Artemis II mission brings Americans together with a shared moment on the moon
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For the first time in over 50 years, NASA launched a crewed mission called Artemis II to orbit the moon. This mission happened at a time when many people in the United States often disagree on many topics.

**What happened** – Four astronauts, including the first Black astronaut and the first woman to travel around the moon, flew around the far side of the moon and returned safely. People across the country watched the launch and journey, including groups like Girl Scouts and visitors at planetariums.

**Impact** – The mission attracted nearly 70% of Americans who said they felt excited about space exploration. It brought together people of all ages, backgrounds, and political views, giving them a rare moment of shared interest and hope. The crew’s diversity also helped many people feel represented and inspired.

**What's next step** – NASA plans to land astronauts on the moon by 2028 and eventually build a permanent base there. Artemis II is seen as the first step in a longer journey to explore space and learn more about our universe.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Artemis II mission united many Americans by showing how space exploration can inspire and bring people together, reminding us of our shared connection to Earth and the moon.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission Artemis II réunit les Américains autour d’un moment partagé sur la Lune
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pour la première fois depuis plus de 50 ans, la NASA a lancé une mission habitée appelée Artemis II pour faire le tour de la Lune. Cette mission a eu lieu à un moment où beaucoup de personnes aux États-Unis sont souvent en désaccord sur de nombreux sujets.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Quatre astronautes, dont le premier astronaute noir et la première femme à voyager autour de la Lune, ont fait le tour de la face cachée de la Lune et sont revenus sains et saufs. Des personnes à travers le pays ont regardé le lancement et le voyage, y compris des groupes comme les Girl Scouts et des visiteurs de planétariums.

**Impact** – La mission a enthousiasmé près de 70 % des Américains. Elle a réuni des personnes de tous âges, origines et opinions politiques, leur offrant un rare moment d’intérêt et d’espoir communs. La diversité de l’équipage a aussi aidé beaucoup à se sentir représentés et inspirés.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA prévoit d’atterrir des astronautes sur la Lune d’ici 2028 et de construire une base permanente. Artemis II est considéré comme le premier pas d’un long voyage pour explorer l’espace et mieux comprendre l’univers.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La mission Artemis II a uni de nombreux Américains en montrant comment l’exploration spatiale peut inspirer et rassembler, rappelant notre lien commun avec la Terre et la Lune.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión Artemis II une a los estadounidenses con un momento compartido en la luna
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Por primera vez en más de 50 años, la NASA lanzó una misión tripulada llamada Artemis II para orbitar la luna. Esta misión ocurrió en un momento en que muchas personas en Estados Unidos suelen estar en desacuerdo sobre muchos temas.

**Qué pasó** – Cuatro astronautas, incluyendo al primer astronauta negro y a la primera mujer en viajar alrededor de la luna, volaron alrededor del lado oculto de la luna y regresaron sanos y salvos. Personas de todo el país vieron el lanzamiento y el viaje, incluidos grupos como las Girl Scouts y visitantes de planetarios.

**Impacto** – La misión emocionó a casi el 70 % de los estadounidenses. Reunió a personas de todas las edades, orígenes y opiniones políticas, dándoles un raro momento de interés y esperanza compartidos. La diversidad de la tripulación también ayudó a muchas personas a sentirse representadas e inspiradas.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA planea aterrizar astronautas en la luna para 2028 y eventualmente construir una base permanente. Artemis II es visto como el primer paso en un viaje más largo para explorar el espacio y aprender más sobre nuestro universo.

**Frase clave** – La misión Artemis II unió a muchos estadounidenses al mostrar cómo la exploración espacial puede inspirar y juntar a las personas, recordándonos nuestra conexión compartida con la Tierra y la luna.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a diverse group of silhouetted figures standing together on Earth’s surface, united beneath a glowing full moon that subtly reflects the Earth’s continents without borders, with a luminous rocket ascending toward the moon’s far side in the starry sky above, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and gentle blues.
